responseSet.documentInfoXML: 199038240-245Psychosomatic Medicine and Contemporary PsychoanalysisBy Graeme J. Taylor. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1987, xiii + 391 pp., $37.50.JamesJ.Strain James J. Strain, M.D.New York, N.Y.
responseSet.title: Psychosomatic Medicine and Contemporary Psychoanalysis: By Graeme J. Taylor.  Madison, Conn.:  Int. Univ. Press, 1987, xiii + 391 pp., $37.50.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychosomatic Medicine and Contemporary Psychoanalysis

By Graeme J. Taylor. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1987, xiii + 391 pp., $37.50.

James Strain M.D.

James Strain

James J. Strain, M.D.
New York, N.Y.

James Strain  

Psychosomatic Medicine and Contemporary Psychoanalysis is actually two books with an important integrating summary. In Part I, Taylor carefully and skillfully reviews the historical underpinnings, the evaluation of concepts, and the current status of psychosomatic medicine in three concise, well-referenced and scholarly chapters: "The Classical Era of Psychosomatic Medicine," "Stressful Life Events and a General Susceptibility to Disease," and "Emotional Expressiveness and the Psychosomatic Process." The author describes the historical origins of alexithymia, the concept, its definition, and its measurement (including critical details about the scales that are currently employed in its assessment). This is an important chapter not only because of the clarity with which the concept of alexithymia is presented, but because it shows how such a "thought disorder" meshed with early psychosomatic theory, becoming a springboard for Taylor's development of a new conceptualization of the etiology of psychosomatic disorders and treatment approaches. He stresses that the intervention with patients who have alexithymia (and psychosomatic illness) is to confront the communication defect, develop affective tolerance, and increase awareness of the unconscious, but avoid the usual analytic schema that tries to elucidate conflict.

Part II describes "Mind-Body Relations in Early Life" with two contributions: "A Revised View of the Mother-Infant Relationship," and "The Transitional Object and disorders of Regulation." The author emphasizes the concept of "psychic organizers," "the mother-infant relationship as a regulatory system," and places special significance on the "hidden regulatory processes in the mother-infant relationship" described by M. A. Hofer's painstaking research using animal models: regulatory systems of heart rate, sleep-wake states, growth, thermo-regulation, and the effects of vestibular stimulation, and their relation to development and attachment. From the review of transitional objects and disorders of regulation between mother and infant, Taylor proposes that there is a need to move from classical analytic theory to self psychology to not only correct psychic defects, but to mitigate the person's vulnerability to physical disease as well.

[[RunningHead]]

Los Fundamentos De La Tecnica Psicoanalitica

By H. Horacio Etchegoyen. Buenos Aires: Amorrtu Editores, 1986, 788 pp. Psychoanalytic literature abounds in articles on technique, but treatises on this topic are scarce. Etchegoyen's comprehensive work is a most recent attempt to fill that relative void.

Jaime Nos M.D.

Jaime Nos

Jaime P. Nos, M.D.
Barcelona, Spain

Jaime Nos  

The author, a distinguished Argentinian analyst, gestated this book out of his experience teaching, since 1970, a course on "Theory of Technique." His stated aim for this work is ambitious: to offer a complete panorama of the current issues and problems of psychoanalytic technique and to review, from a historical perspective, the development of the different theoretical lines which inform those issues and problems. Etchegoyen sees theory and technique being indisolubly articulated: psychoanalytic technique determines our observational method and, therefore, the data which we gather, and vice versa, psychoanalytic praxis, are always grounded in theory.

245

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 199038821-828The Freudian Metaphor: Toward Paradigm Change in PsychoanalysisBy Donald P. Spence. New York: Norton, 1987, 230 pp., $22.95.MarkJ.Gehrie Mark J. Gehrie, Ph.D.Chicago, Illinois
responseSet.title: The Freudian Metaphor: Toward Paradigm Change in Psychoanalysis: By Donald P. Spence.  New York:  Norton, 1987, 230 pp., $22.95.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Freudian Metaphor: Toward Paradigm Change in Psychoanalysis

By Donald P. Spence. New York: Norton, 1987, 230 pp., $22.95.

Mark Gehrie Ph.D.

Mark Gehrie

Mark J. Gehrie, Ph.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Mark Gehrie  

The Freudian Metaphor raises critical issues in the epistemology of psychoanalysis which go beyond clarification or elaboration of basic principles, and also reflect fundamental divisions within the field and among its practitioners. Although Spence focuses exclusively on the epistemological consequences and implications for the discipline, I believe that the role of psychoanalytic education and the concept of the field in the minds of its primary carriers of knowledge—clinical psychoanalysts—is also very much at issue.

Jerome Brunner's Foreword provides an excellent summary introduction to the major point which Spence spends the book elaborating: that "objectivity" in psychoanalysis is not possible, or even a meaningful technical goal, because "… we have come to appreciate the degree to which psychological processes are bound by the contexts in which they occur" (p. xv). This fundamental relativism lies in direct contrast to the powerfully held view that somehow psychoanalysis can be made more scientific, that if only we can cook the soft subjectivities out of our theory and method, we can achieve a disciplinary status not unlike that of chemistry or physics. Bruner boldly and correctly confronts this view: "To deny the difficulties of interpretation by making claims to a scientific status is to weaken the central claims of psychoanalysis as a human science. In a deep sense, the difficulties of contemporary psychoanalysis inhere precisely in its claim to being a 'science' in the positivistic sense of the term and by avoiding the thorny issues of interpretation and hermeneutics" (p. xv, italics added).

821

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 199038836-839Freud Reappraised: A Fresh Look at Psychoanalytic TheoryBy Robert R. Holt. New York: Guilford Press, 1989, 433 pp., $40.00.RobertS.Wallerstein Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.San Francisco, California
responseSet.title: Freud Reappraised: A Fresh Look at Psychoanalytic Theory: By Robert R. Holt.  New York:  Guilford Press, 1989, 433 pp., $40.00.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud Reappraised: A Fresh Look at Psychoanalytic Theory

By Robert R. Holt. New York: Guilford Press, 1989, 433 pp., $40.00.

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
San Francisco, California

Robert Wallerstein  

The ego-psychological metapsychology paradigm created as a development upon, and from, Freud's legacy, by Hartmann, Rapaport, Kris, Loewenstein, Jacobson, and a host of others, was both the hallmark and the apotheosis of psychoanalysis in America during those immediate post World War II decades when the American Psychoanalytic Association, swelled by (and to a considerable degree, dominated by) the recent transplanted tide of refugee colleagues fleeing Hitler's march across Europe, represented the majority in analysis worldwide, both in numbers and the most vigorous centers of its growth and dissemination. Within that galaxy David Rapaport stood out as the great and relentless systematizer of the metapsychological edifice, dedicated, via detailed and critical (and Talmudic) exegesis, to articulating all the component elements of a sprawling theoretical structure (or structures) into a logically comprehensive explanatory whole, which, with the filling in of some obvious gaps—the lack still of a truly psychoanalytic learning theory being a major one—could realize the ego psychologists' hegemonic dream of extending Freudian psychoanalysis from a drive- and conflict-based psychology developed from and explaining the psychopathological phenomena of the analytic consulting rooms into a transcendentally encompassing general psychology explaining the totality of human behavior, normal and abnormal, in all its ramifications.

Robert Holt was one of an extraordinary band of colleagues and students of Rapaport's (M. M Gill, R. Schafer, M. Brenman, and G. S. Klein being others) who were drawn to that vision and that task.

836

responseSet.title: Der Fall Hermine Hug-Hellmuth: Eine Geschichte Der Frühen Kinderpsychoanalyse. [the Case of Hermine Hug-Hellmuth: A History of Early Child Psychoanalysis]: By Angela Graf-Nold.  Munich & Vienna:  Verlag Int. Psychoanal., 1988, 320 pp., $35.00.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Der Fall Hermine Hug-Hellmuth: Eine Geschichte Der Frühen Kinderpsychoanalyse. [the Case of Hermine Hug-Hellmuth: A History of Early Child Psychoanalysis]

By Angela Graf-Nold. Munich & Vienna: Verlag Int. Psychoanal., 1988, 320 pp., $35.00.

George Maclean M.D. and Ulrich Rappen M.D.

Ulrich Rappen

George Maclean, M.D.
Ottawa
Ulrich Rappen, M.D.
Toronto

Ulrich Rappen  

Within a short period of time, several authors have written excellent biographies of the most notable pioneers in the history of psychoanalysis. These have included the benchmark, scholarly biography of Freud by Peter Gay (1988). Many others have been published, Roazen's (1985) biography of Helene Deutch, Grosskurth's (1986) biography of Melanie Klein, Quinn's (1987) biography of Karen Horney, and Young-Bruehl's (1988) biography of Anna Freud. It is of interest to see a book published about the more enigmatic, less known pioneer of child psychoanalysis, Hermine Hug-Hellmuth.

Even the title of the book arouses some interest, as it avoids stating exactly what the book is about, leaving the author with an open field. Is it a biography of the first practicing child psychoanalyst? Perhaps it is more specifically the description of a criminal case? Possibly it is even grander, an actual history of child psychoanalysis? If it were it would be the first ever published. Repeated reading left us dissatisfied. The book is neither a biography, nor the description of a case, nor a work of history. It is a curious mélange of fact, fiction, and fantasy.

Writing a biography is difficult. One problem is maintaining a proper literary and psychological distance from the subject of the biography. When one has invested years in research and writing to produce a book about a person, it is easy to idealize the subject of one's interest. Grosskurth (1986) idealizes Melanie Klein. Young-Bruehl (1988) exceeds Grosskurth by elevating Anna Freud to the level of being at least superhuman, at most supernatural. Gay (1988), Roazen (1985), and Quinn (1987) manage to maintain scholarship and distance, where the subjects of their biographies, respectively Sigmund Freud, Helene Deutsch and Karen Horney, remain human.

839

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Essential Papers on Borderline Disorders: One Hundered Years at the Border

Edited by Michael H. Stone. New York: N.Y. Univ. Press, 1986, 580 pp., $55.00 (hardcover), $25.00 (paperback).

Martin Willick M.D.

Martin Willick

Martin S. Willick, M.D.
Teaneck, New Jersey

Martin Willick  

This book is one of a series of Essential Papers in psychoanalysis which has included volumes on depression, narcissism, psychoses, and object relations. In each instance, it was no easy task for the editor to select

842

the most essential papers written about the topic. This is especially true for the current volume. Stone was faced with the task of discriminating among a vast number of papers, many of which have appeared in the psychoanalytic literature over the past 20 years stimulated by O. F. Kernberg's pioneering effort to apply psychoanalytic theories of development and psychopathology to this important group of disorders. The editor's task was even more difficult because the borderline group of illnesses has proved to be rather heterogeneous, necessitating choices from a number of different psychoanalytic points of view. Stone has added to his own burden of selection by deciding, correctly, to include contributions from the field of general psychiatry with which he believes psychoanalysts ought to be acquainted.

The result is an excellent volume which should be in the library of any clinician interested in current thinking as well as the evolution of the concept of the borderline group of disorders. What makes this book truly worthwhile is not so much the papers themselves, but Stone's illuminating, comprehensive, and comprehensible overview, which constitutes over fifty pages in itself. In similar anthologies one often finds a brief introduction to each paper, but in this volume the editor provides us with an extraordinarily perceptive narrative account of the still changing nature of the concept of borderline conditions.

Stone introduces each of the chronological sections with an account of the major themes to emerge during the two decades or so which are covered. He is particularly well qualified for this task because he is equally at home with the literature from the fields of general psychiatry and psychoanalysis.

In the first section, which covers the 1880's through the 1920's, Stone outlines the attempt on the part of both psychoanalysts and descriptive psychiatrists to cope with a category of illness lying somewhere between the already existing concepts of neurosis and psychosis. However, the three papers in this section, written by the descriptive psychiatrists J. C. Prichard, C. H. Hughes, and I. C. Rosse, do not provide a useful background to the borderline topic. It might have been preferable to reproduce some of the more relevant comments by J. Falret and E. Bleuler which Stone mentions in his commentary.

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 199038831-836Guilt and Desire: Religious Attitudes and their Pathological DerivativesBy Antoine Vergote (trans. M. H. Wood). New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1988, 254 pp., $27.75.ThomasAcklin Thomas Acklin, Ph.D.Latrobe, Pennsylvania
responseSet.title: Guilt and Desire: Religious Attitudes and their Pathological Derivatives: By Antoine Vergote (trans. M. H. Wood).  New Haven:  Yale Univ. Press, 1988, 254 pp., $27.75.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guilt and Desire: Religious Attitudes and their Pathological Derivatives

By Antoine Vergote (trans. M. H. Wood). New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1988, 254 pp., $27.75.

Thomas Acklin Ph.D.

Thomas Acklin

Thomas Acklin, Ph.D.
Latrobe, Pennsylvania

Thomas Acklin  

Have psychoanalytic insight and interpretation of religious experience

831

and religious phenomena been overly circumspect or at times even prejudicial? Carrying his analysis of religious phenomena farther than perhaps any psychoanalytically inspired work, Antoine Vergote scrutinizes instances of religious experience drawn from such varied sources as classics of the Christian mystical tradition, clinical material, and autobiographical and historical documents.

Antoine Vergote is Professor Emeritus of the University of Louvain, Belgium, where he taught in the faculties of psychology, philosophy, and theology. He is founder of the Center for Psychology of Religion at that university, and is a practicing psychoanalyst and past president of the Belgian School of Psychoanalysis. Vergote has published several books and numerous articles in the area of psychology of religion, some of which have been translated into as many as five languages. Guilt and Desire was originally published in French in 1978. In the opinion of this reviewer, who completed doctoral studies under Vergote's direction, it is Vergote's most important work.

While maintaining the neutrality of psychoanalytic listening, Vergote accords the religious phenomena he studies their own autonomy and respects their proper context. His bipartite study considers the consciousness of evil and of guilt, and the vicissitudes of desire. Guilt and desire, as Vergote says, are two words charged with an uncanny, disquieting familiarity, summoning up two basic dimensions of human existence which are at the heart of both religion and psychoanalysis. They are the two axes he pursues, corresponding to the two tendencies Freud analyzed as a propensity to the obsessional or to the hysterical neurosis. Nonetheless, Vergote's analysis of various forms of psychopathology that draw upon religious symbolism is not restricted to classical neurotic phenomena. For example, it takes into account what he calls "malignant obsession," a sort of obsessional psychosis, as well as other forms of psychotic splitting and destructuralization of reality to be found in the schizophrenias, including the paranoid forms.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dream, 4000 Years of Theory and Practice

By Nancy Parcifal-Charles. West Cornwall, Conn.: Locust Hill Press, 1986, vi + 576 pp., 2 vols., sold as set only, $59.95.

David Hawkins M.D.

David Hawkins

David R. Hawkins, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

David Hawkins  

The Dream is a critical, annotated bibliography which deals with over 700 books and monographs. It spans 4000 years and is based on publications not only in English, but also in other European languages. The author has tried to include all important books or monographs on the dream published up to and including March 1986. The organization is alphabetical, by author. There is a comprehensive subject index and name index.

The author gives her own critical evaluation of the works. As can be inferred from the above description, each book receives relatively short treatment. Freud's The Interpretation of Dreams is handled in just under four pages. The author does call this, "the most important book ever published on dreams," however, she goes on to say, "Contrary to popular misconception, Freud's The Interpretation of Dreams is not exceptional for its exposition of a startlingly new theory of dreams, but rather as a brilliant synthesis of many fragmented speculations and much research done by others prior to and even during the writing of his book."

820

responseSet.title: The Transference Neurosis in Freud's Writings
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Transference Neurosis in Freud's Writings

Gail Reed Ph.D.

Gail Reed

Gail S. Reed, Ph.D.
1199 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Gail Reed  

ABSTRACT

This paper reviews the evolution of the concept of transference neurosis in Freud's writings. It suggests that the language in which the concept of the transference neurosis is originally expressed by Freud includes an idea of the analyst as aggressively pursuing the analytic cure by waging a solitary battle against the patient's disease. With the representation of the death drive and the larger role accorded to sadism as its external manifestation in Freud's revised drive theory of 1920, the patient becomes the ally; resistance, in the sense of the conservative forces, not disease, in the sense of libidinal conflict, becomes the enemy. It is thus difficult to speak of a transference neurosis in the circumscribed way Freud originally meant it, and he ceased to use the term after 1926 rather than redefine it to fit his broader perspective. In this broader perspective, relative resolution of conflict replaced radical liberation of the patient from disease.

That Freud did not redefine the term does not imply that he discarded it, or that we necessarily should. This paper suggests that Freud implied a functional distinction between transference as transforming agent and transference neurosis as result of that transformation. That distinction defines psychoanalytic cure in terms of the understanding of a symbolic transformation which is, through the transference neurosis, reexperienced as part of the psychoanalytic process.

responseSet.title: Effects of Theory on Psychoanalytic Technique and on the Development of Psychoanalytic Process
responseSet.authorMast: Herbert J. Schlesinger and  Eleanor Schuker
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Effects of Theory on Psychoanalytic Technique and on the Development of Psychoanalytic Process

Herbert Schlesinger Ph.D. and Eleanor Schuker M.D.

Eleanor Schuker

Herbert J. Schlesinger, Ph.D.
Eleanor Schuker, M.D.
150 West End Avenue (26A)
New York, NY 10023

Eleanor Schuker  

What are the effects of the theoretical stance of the analyst on his or her psychoanalytic technique and on the development of psychoanalytic process in the earliest phase of an analysis? To explore this question, panelists representing differing theoretical stances and the audience had access to an edited, confidential transcript covering the first four hours of an analysis. Schlesinger suggested that the panelists apply their own theoretical orientations to the clinical data to demonstrate how an analysis might ideally develop and to differentiate their own approach. The clinical material was selected because it represented the beginning of an analysis of a patient who seemed "suitable" enough, the analyst's behavior in the first week seemed generally facilitative, and the little history that had been taken was obtained by the referring colleague, so that the transcript reflected the earliest meeting of patient and analyst.

The patient was a married mother of two, in her late twenties, who worked part-time in publishing. Referred to a consultant analyst by her internist because of mild somatic complaints and a sense of unhappiness and dissatisfaction in her marriage, she then was referred to the treating analyst, Dr. Smith. The transcript of the four sessions included an initial discussion of the patient's complaints and feelings about accepting help. She began on the couch in the second session, and presented her first dreams in the third session.

Schlesinger began by asking the panelists to discuss what theory might be guiding the analyst and to demonstrate how the analysis might ideally develop from their own point of view.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Montreal, Canada, May 7, 1988.

Panelists: Robert Wallerstein, Roger A. Dufresne, Otto Kernberg, Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber.

221

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Practice: Vol. 1. Principles

By Helmut Thomä and Horst Kächele. Heidelberg & Berlin: Springer Verlag, 1985, XXVI + 421 pp.

Leon Wurmser M.D.

Leon Wurmser

Leon Wurmser, M.D.
Towson, Maryland

Leon Wurmser  

Thomä, Kächele, and their coworkers have very decisively contributed to giving the new German psychoanalysis a standing of high relevance in the ongoing psychoanalytic discourse—by their courageous insistence on far greater scientific accountability and with that of a much deepened epistemological examination of claims and premises of psychoanalytic practice and theory. When they presented this first volume of their synthesis of analytic experience and thought in its German version, it was immediately a dramatic success in Europe, and considered a most impressive reintegration of the insights of the first nine decades of psychoanalysis—the more important as it appeared at a time of a serious crisis in psychoanalysis, in the United States and increasingly also in Europe, especially with regard to its organizational, sociopolitical, and scientific credentials. A nonpolemical treatment of many of the problems underlying this crisis is overdue: "We discern within psychoanalysis many attempts at integration, or at least serious scientific endeavors to resolve differences of opinion more clearly than before" (p. xxv).

I shall try to present critically the three main points of this first volume that serve this goal.

"Our leitmotif is the conviction that the analyst's contribution to the

responseSet.title: Foundations of Psychoanalysis Reconsidered
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Foundations of Psychoanalysis Reconsidered

W. Meissner S.J.

W. Meissner

W. W. Meissner, S.J.
129 Mount Auburn Street
Combridge, MA 02138

W. Meissner  

ABSTRACT

Grünbaum's approach to psychoanalysis suffers from several difficulties. it imposes a standard of logical reductionism and methodological purity that not only violates the nature of psychoanalytic knowledge, but imposes an invalid standard of verification and scientific confirmation. It utilizes a brand of dichotomous reasoning that forces psychoanalytic propositions into artificial positions that do not reflect the actuality of analytic practice. It imposes a standard of verification that is impossible for psychoanalysis, along with all forms of psychological knowledge, to reach. It visualizes psychoanalysis as encompassing only one form of knowledge of human psychic life, forcing it into a model that eliminates other aspects of the psychoanalytic process, so that psychoanalysis is subjected to criticism only on one dimension among several—a kind of psychoanalytic straw man. The psychoanalysis that is so impaled often is difficult for the psychoanalytic practitioner to recognize. To the extent that Grünbaum's skillful and highly informed criticism of the philosophical bases of psychoanalysis encounters these difficulties, the value of his argument falls short of providing a useful basis for advancing psychoanalytic knowledge and particularly for promoting the quest for pertinent standards of validation within psychoanalysis.

David Fisher  

This is a marvelous and disturbing book. Ostensibly, it is an accurate, chronological account of the main contributors to crowd psychology. But it is much more than an intellectual history chronicling theoreticians of mass society; it is a reminder about the extent to which we are all easily dominated by the techniques of mass manipulation.

responseSet.title: Culture and Human Nature: Theoretical Papers of Melford E. Spiro: Edited by Benjamin Kilborne and L. L. Langness.  Chicago:  Univ. Chicago Press, 1987, 309 pp., $39.95 (hard cover); $14.95 (paperback).
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Culture and Human Nature: Theoretical Papers of Melford E. Spiro

Edited by Benjamin Kilborne and L. L. Langness. Chicago: Univ. Chicago Press, 1987, 309 pp., $39.95 (hard cover); $14.95 (paperback).

Aaron Esman M.D.

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman, M.D.
New York, N.Y.

Aaron Esman  

To a large degree contemporary anthropology has turned away from the "culture and personality" studies that represented a major trend a generation ago, toward concerns with more "objective" factors in the shaping of culture—the roles of ecology, technology, climate, and the like. In the process, psychoanalytic concepts have played a relatively small role in recent anthropological thought; Totem and Taboo has been written off as a "phylogenetic fantasy," and the universality of the Oedipus complex has been widely challenged, seen as an artifact of the patriarchal culture of 19th-century Europe.

It is refreshing, therefore, to find in the work of Melford Spiro a vigorous reassertion of the value—indeed the necessity—of an appreciation of psychoanalytic ideas for the understanding of cultural phenomena. Spiro, Professor of Anthropology at The University of California, San Diego, has devoted a lifetime of research to the study of family structures and religious institutions in a variety of cultures, particularly in Israel and East Asia. The present volume contains a distillation of his work, in the form of papers prepared for and initially published in various journals and collections, assembled here by two of his former students.

The recurrent theme in these essays, played in many variations, is Spiro's insistence on the existence of what he calls "pan-human" characteristics—universal features of human nature based on man's biological essence—that interdigitate with social and cultural realities to shape personality development and cultural institutions. He rejects the popular notion of culture as an "unmoved mover," the cultural relativism that denies universal, generically human behavioral propensities.

237

responseSet.title: Assessment of Suitability for Low-Fee Control Psychoanalysis: Some Theoretical and Technical Considerations
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Assessment of Suitability for Low-Fee Control Psychoanalysis: Some Theoretical and Technical Considerations

Robert Epstein M.D.

Robert Epstein

Robert A. Epstein, M.D.
2702 Dana Street
Berkeley, CA 94705

Robert Epstein  

ABSTRACT

In the assessment of suitabilty for low-fee control analysis, various factors may interfere with successful referral, evaluation, and disposition. Variables within the referring analyst, patient, candidate, and supervisor are examined in their interaction with the circumstances of the assessment enterprise. Issues of competence, self-esteem, and oedipal rivalry, as well as guilt feelings, concerns about rejection, and other conflicts may be mobilized and may prevent a successful outcome. Clinical data from three assessments are presented and discussed. The concept of an assessment process, analogous to the psychoanalytic process, is proposed. In this process, manifestations of the patient's intrapsychic life and indicators of potential transferences emerge in the context of the analysis of the patient's reactions to the assessment. Potential sources of interference with the assessment process are identified, and measures to avoid them are suggested.

responseSet.title: Evaluation and Diagnosis of Sexual Abuse of Little Girls
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Evaluation and Diagnosis of Sexual Abuse of Little Girls

Susan Sherkow M.D.

Susan Sherkow

Susan P. Sherkow, M.D.
300 Central Park West (ID)
New York, NY 10024

Susan Sherkow  

ABSTRACT

In recent years, increasing evidence of sexual abuse of children has created a new field of medicolegal inquiry requiring a new expertise. In psychoanalysis, one of our specific tasks is to address the issues of diagnosis and of the pathogenic effect of sexual abuse as it is ultimately reflected in the intrapsychic dynamics of the developing ego. My hypothesis is that it may be possible to determine early in the treatment whether sexual abuse has taken place. The case of a two-and-three-quarters-year-old girl, suspected of having been molested by her father, is presented in depth. The evolution of material confirming the diagnosis is presented by way of documenting evidence of sexual abuse. Issues elaborating the difficulty in making the diagnosis are also explored. Finally, a synthesis of diagnostic considerations useful for arriving at a diagnosis of sexual abuse is presented in the hope that these factors will serve as diagnostic guidelines.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Case Study: Toward a Method for Systematic Inquiry
responseSet.authorMast: Enrico E. Jones and  Michael Windholz
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Case Study: Toward a Method for Systematic Inquiry

Enrico Jones Ph.D. and Michael Windholz Ph.D.

Michael Windholz

Enrico E. Jones, Ph.D.
Michael Windholz, Ph.D.
Department of Psychology University of California at Berkeley
Berkeley, CA 94720

Michael Windholz  

ABSTRACT

This study represents a contribution toward the systematic and empirical investigation of psychoanalytic treatments. The method used, the Q-technique, allows the transformation of clinical data into a form amenable to quantitative analysis, thereby providing an empirical means to test theoretically and clinically derived understandings of psychoanalytic process. The treatment hours of a six-year analysis were audio-recorded and transcribed, and blocks of ten sessions were selected at regular intervals throughout the course of the analysis. Transcripts of these hours were then rated in random fashion by clinical judges with a Q-set designed to provide a standard language for the description and classification of analytic process. These descriptions of analytic hours, as structured by the Q-set, proved highly reliable, demonstrating the method's promise for addressing the long-standing problem of achieving reliable clinical judgments. Results suggest that subjecting the traditional psychoanalytic case study to systematic inquiry can contribute to establishing an empirical science base for some psychoanalytic propositions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Four Psychoanalytic Perspectives: A Study of Differences in Interpretive Interventions

Sidney Fine M.D. and Esther Fine M.S.

Esther Fine

Sidney Fine, M.D.
Esther Fine, M.S.W.
1800 Fairburn Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90025

Esther Fine  

ABSTRACT

This research study was designed to test two main hypotheses: (1) there are detectable differences in interpretive style among classical, Kleinian, Kohutian, and Kernbergian analysts; and (2) the nature of these differences can be discerned and described. The methodology consists of three phases of data collection. The statistical findings are presented, along with clinical examples, followed by a discussion of their implications.

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="APA" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="APA.038.0605A" ISSN="0003-0651"><artyear>1990</artyear><artvol actual="38">38</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">605-636</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Helping Patients by Analyzing Self-Criticism</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Kris, Anton O." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Anton</nfirst><nmid>O.</nmid><nlast>Kris</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Anton O. Kris, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>37 Philbrick Road</ln><ln>Brookline, MA 02146</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Helping Patients by Analyzing Self-Criticism
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Helping Patients by Analyzing Self-Criticism

Anton Kris M.D.

Anton Kris

Anton O. Kris, M.D.
37 Philbrick Road
Brookline, MA 02146

Anton Kris  

ABSTRACT

This paper is addressed to patients' need for help with punitive self-critical attitudes. Such help has not always been sufficiently provided by psychoanalysts, owing to an unrecognized failure of neutrality. Historically, a gradual overemphasis on the concept of an unconscious sense of guilt has acted as a barrier to the appreciation of shame. An alternative concept, punitive unconscious self-criticism, which stands in contrast to constructive self-criticism and is common to the painful affects of guilt, shame, humiliation, and depression, can facilitate helpful analytic treatment. Heinz Kohut's contributions are examined. His analytic stance is differentiated from his theories of development. In the former, characterized by an affirmative attitude, he takes a position of functional neutrality toward shame and pays consistent though unstated attention to the effects of punitive unconscious self-criticism. The affirmative attitude can be employed without adoption of Kohut's self psychology, i.e., without abandoning the basic psychoanalytic approach to mental conflict and development. The concept of punitive unconscious self-criticism and the concept of divergent conflict, provide sufficient explanatory power. Clinical examples are used to illustrate these ideas.

[[RunningHead]]

The Nature of Therapeutic Action in Psychoanalysis

Paul Gray M.D.

Paul Gray

Paul Gray, M.D.
3315 Wisconsin Avenue N.W.
Washington, DC 20016

Paul Gray  

ABSTRACT

The nature of therapeutic action varies not only with each patient's psychological predilection for utilizing opportunities for change, but also with the manner in which the analyst or therapist presents opportunity for change. Ideally, the therapist bases the latter on personally identifiable theoretical concepts and aims. This inquiry focuses on a relatively narrow field of clinical material in order to provide as close as possible an examination of therapeutic action. The technical approach provides opportunities for change, minimizing alterations resulting from internalizing processes or suggestion. The therapeutic actions arise from analyst and patient sharing observations of the patient's intrapsychic activities of resistance to drive derivatives the patient briefly allowed into consciousness, and represent processes of ego maturation set in motion by intellectually gained and experientially exercised insights.
